quick_facts.label: Recognised professional area
quick_facts.value: Medical radiology and imaging is listed under the Medical Radiology, Imaging and Therapeutic Technology Professional category in India’s National Commission for Allied and Healthcare Professions (NCAHP) framework; the NCAHP portal identifies Radiology and Imaging Technologist as a profession within that category.

quick_facts.label: Usual entry qualification
quick_facts.value: A common institutional requirement is Class 12 / 10+2 with Physics, Chemistry and Biology (PCB); minimum marks, English requirements, age criteria and accepted equivalents vary by university.

quick_facts.label: Usual duration
quick_facts.value: Many programmes are structured as three academic years plus a one-year internship (four years total), although titles and programme structures differ among institutions.

quick_facts.label: Scope of training
quick_facts.value: The degree prepares learners for technical diagnostic-imaging work involving modalities such as conventional and digital X-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound, fluoroscopy and mammography, with attention to radiation safety, quality control and patient care.

quick_facts.label: Professional enrolment status
quick_facts.value: As of the NCAHP enrolment portal information reviewed, allied-health-professional enrolment is described as data collection rather than registration; the portal says registration regulations are under process.

sections.heading: Eligibility: science at Class 12 is the usual route
paragraphs: For direct entry, applicants should normally expect to need a recognised 10+2 / senior-secondary qualification with Physics, Chemistry and Biology. Some institutions additionally require English, a specified aggregate or individual passing marks, and minimum age requirements; applicants should therefore check the exact prospectus for the institution and admission year.

The National Commission for Allied and Healthcare Professions Act, 2021 defines an allied health professional to include a technician or technologist supporting diagnosis and treatment, with a qualifying diploma or degree of at least 2,000 hours delivered across two to four years. This statutory definition is a framework for allied-health qualifications, not a substitute for an individual university’s admission rules.
bullets: Potential additional conditions: English, minimum marks, age, medical fitness, category documentation and university-specific equivalence rules.

Lateral entry may be available at some institutions for holders of an appropriate post-10+2 medical-imaging diploma; for example, MAHE lists a lateral B.Sc. Medical Imaging Technology route based on a two-year diploma and specified marks.

sections.heading: Duration: generally four years when internship is included
paragraphs: The common full-time pattern is three years of coursework followed by a 12-month clinical internship, making four years in total. Institutions may describe this as B.Sc. Medical Imaging Technology, BMRIT, B.Sc. Medical Radiology and Imaging Technology, or a closely related title, so applicants should verify both the award title and whether internship is embedded or separately scheduled.

The statutory NCAHP Act describes allied-health diploma or degree qualifications as spanning two to four years and at least 2,000 hours, while individual BMRIT/MIT programmes commonly publish a four-year structure including internship.
bullets: A published example from JSS Academy: 6 semesters plus 12 months of internship.
A published example from Father Muller: 3 years plus 1 year of internship.
Confirm clinical-posting and internship arrangements before accepting an offer, especially where a course page lists only academic semesters.

sections.heading: Curriculum: imaging science, safe practice and supervised clinical learning
paragraphs: Published curricula combine health-science foundations with imaging-specific theory and practical work. Core foundations commonly include anatomy, physiology, biochemistry, pathology, microbiology and pharmacology; imaging content commonly covers radiographic physics, positioning and procedures, image processing, radiation biology and protection, quality control, and patient-care considerations.

Training normally extends across multiple diagnostic modalities rather than only conventional radiography. Published programme descriptions include X-ray and digital imaging, fluoroscopy, mammography, ultrasonography, CT, MRI, Doppler/DSA, PACS and department planning. The precise mix of modalities, clinical exposure and assessment is institution-specific.
bullets: Foundational sciences: anatomy, physiology, pathology and related biomedical subjects.
Professional imaging skills: positioning, radiographic techniques, image processing and equipment operation.
Safety and quality: radiation protection, radiobiology, quality assurance and quality control.
Advanced/modality exposure: CT, MRI, ultrasound, fluoroscopy, mammography and digital imaging/PACS, where offered.

sections.heading: Admission: process is determined by the university, state or Union Territory
paragraphs: There is no single admission route stated across all B.Sc. medical-radiology/imaging programmes. Universities may use qualifying-examination merit, their own entrance assessment and interview, or another process permitted by the relevant institution and jurisdiction. Applicants should rely on the current official prospectus rather than assuming that NEET-UG, CUET, or any one examination is universally required.

A 2026 BMRIT admission page from Symbiosis Institute of Health Sciences illustrates an institution-specific process: online registration, screening, entrance test and personal interview, document verification, and payment of the first fee instalment. Its published selection formula gives equal weight to senior-secondary performance and entrance examination/personal interaction; this is an example, not a national rule.
bullets: Check the programme’s current eligibility, selection method, document list, reservation policy and clinical-training arrangement.
Use the official university or state/UT admission notice for dates and deadlines, which can change during an admission cycle.
For foreign qualifications, check equivalence requirements and the applicable institution/state allied-health authority guidance.

sections.heading: Costs: compare the current institution-specific fee schedule
paragraphs: Fees are not standardised nationally and may exclude or separately charge for registration, examination, hostel, transport, uniforms, clinical materials, deposits and other charges. As dated examples for the 2026–27 academic year, Dayananda Sagar University lists ₹1,72,000 total annual fees for its B.Sc. Medical Radiology & Imaging Technology, while Symbiosis Institute of Health Sciences lists first-year academic fees of ₹1,74,000 in two instalments plus a ₹20,000 refundable institute deposit for BMRIT. These figures are contextual examples, not an India-wide fee benchmark.

Before applying, request the complete current fee sheet and determine whether it states tuition only or the full payable amount, whether internship fees are included, and which deposits or charges are refundable.
bullets: Dated fee example — Dayananda Sagar University, 2026–27: ₹1,72,000 annual total shown for B.Sc. Medical Radiology & Imaging Technology.
Dated fee example — Symbiosis Institute of Health Sciences, 2026–27: ₹1,74,000 first-year academic fee shown in two instalments, plus a ₹20,000 refundable deposit.
Fee schedules, scholarships, concessions and refund terms can change by institution and admission cycle.

sections.heading: Next options: postgraduate imaging study or employment-oriented specialisation
paragraphs: After the bachelor’s degree and required internship or experience under the relevant institution’s rules, graduates can pursue postgraduate study in medical radiology and imaging technology. MAHE’s 2026 postgraduate eligibility material lists Master of Medical Radiology and Imaging Technology pathways in CT Imaging Technology, MR Imaging Technology and Breast Imaging Technology, accepting BMRIT/BMRDIT/B.Sc. Medical Imaging Technology or specified equivalent backgrounds subject to its conditions.

Career pathways stated by universities include work in hospitals and diagnostic centres, imaging-industry roles, healthcare IT/PACS and research settings. The NCAHP framework categorises radiology and imaging technologists—including diagnostic medical radiographers, MRI and CT personnel, mammographers and diagnostic medical sonographers—within the medical-radiology, imaging and therapeutic-technology category.
bullets: Postgraduate option: M.MRIT/M.Sc.-level imaging programmes, subject to the receiving university’s eligibility rules.
Practice settings: hospital radiology departments, diagnostic centres, imaging-technology industry, PACS/healthcare IT and research environments.
Verify local employer requirements, state rules and any future NCAHP registration requirements before relying on a particular job pathway.
